data.body:
CONTAINER REVIEW — G4 (child consistency) — parent 5fd2-a7c2-0aec-48fa × this child ce75. 7-mode cross-child interaction sweep; high-confidence + benign-reading filter applied.

VERDICT: ONE high-confidence consistency finding (mode: CONFLICTING ASSUMPTIONS + SCOPE OVERLAP). The remaining 5 modes are clean. The finding is a registry-hygiene/consistency one, NOT a coverage gap (G3 separately confirmed no uncovered parent criterion).

FINDING — child's premise contradicts the parent's settled design, and its scope is fully owned by two siblings (zero net contribution)
- criteria: [G4]
- location: child ce75 §Why + §Scope ("A gate with no off-switch is a single point of failure"; "a documented rollback / back-out path"; "system-level kill switch"; "Deploy ordering for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + signature events") vs parent 5fd2 Success Criterion #4 + §"Design summary"/§"Design decisions revisited (round 5)" + the "rollback is config-off" clarification.
- evidence:
  1. CONFLICTING ASSUMPTIONS (the dominant mode). Parent SC#4 states the OPPOSITE of this child's premise verbatim: "'rollback' is simply turning the preference off — an ordinary config preference, NOT a deploy-time kill-switch, and no separate rollback mechanism is needed." The parent's round-5 settled-decisions section and the Design summary both reaffirm config-off AS the rollback. ce75 asserts the gate "has no off-switch" and demands a "system-level kill switch" + a separate "rollback / back-out path" — i.e. it presupposes a gap the parent design closed by construction. This is a direct premise contradiction between a child and its parent.
  2. CONFIRMED-SUPERSEDED. ce75's own resolution comment (Joe's adjudication) concedes the contradiction: "config-gating IS the kill-switch/rollback ... No separate rollback mechanism is needed ... advisory (not block) over-call the Pass-2 verifier's 'no-existing-mitigation' check should have softened." FORCE_CLOSE with NO signature. So the child delivers nothing and stands only as a superseded over-call.
  3. SCOPE OVERLAP (no net contribution). Every lane ce75 §Scope names is already owned by a sibling:
     - "disable-the-gate config / gate off => claim behaves exactly as today (fail-open)" (ce75 AC#1) is delivered by sibling 092b-3bb7-4890-4cb9 — added AC "ENFORCE the config preference (defaults OFF): off → claim/transition unchanged; on → the fast signature check gates them" (verify-completion PASS; config.require_plan_review_for_claim default False, interface-tested). The config TOOL/UX is separate epic 55de (parent marks it out of scope).
     - "old+new coexistence during rollout (older clones preserve-and-ignore the new events)" + "Deploy ordering for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + signature events" (ce75 AC#2/AC#3 lane) is delivered by sibling db7b-c8fd-190f-4506 — added AC "ROLLOUT / cross-clone coexistence: preserved-and-ignored-by-older-clones, fsck WARNs on newer event types, readers-before-writers" (verify-completion PASS; grounded in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES + is_unknown_newer_type).
- scenarios: A reviewer scanning the registry sees ce75's title ("T12 gap the gate found on itself") and could infer the parent carries an unaddressed rollback/kill-switch obligation that contradicts SC#4, or that ce75 and 092b/db7b both own the disable-config + rollout-ordering lane (overlap/double-ownership). Neither should stand: SC#4 settles the premise, and 092b + db7b own the residual mechanics.
- impact: Consistency / registry-hygiene only — no live ordering gap and no uncovered parent criterion. The risk is that the child's framing reads as a contradiction of the parent's settled SC#4 and as a scope duplicate of 092b/db7b; left unlinked it muddies which child owns the gate-off preference and the event rollout-ordering.
- checklist_item: "[ ] Reconcile ce75's superseded framing with parent SC#4 (config-off IS the rollback; no separate kill-switch) and de-overlap its scope: gate-off-preference enforcement is owned by 092b, sidecar+signature rollout-ordering/coexistence by db7b — link ce75 superseded_by/relates_to 092b + db7b so the supersession + single-owner trail is explicit rather than only in the close comment."
- suggested_fix: Leave ce75 closed-superseded (correct disposition). Add explicit superseded_by/relates_to links from ce75 → 092b (config-off enforcement) and ce75 → db7b (sidecar+signature rollout-ordering / old-new coexistence) so the registry shows the contradiction was resolved-by-parent-design and the residual lanes are singly owned. No parent-criterion remediation is required (SC#4 + 092b + db7b already cover the lane).

7-MODE SWEEP (the other 5 — clean):
- Implicit shared state: the only shared artifact ce75 touches is the gate-disable config flag, which is the same single flag 092b enforces (no SECOND, conflicting flag is introduced) — no implicit/uncoordinated shared state. CLEAN.
- Dependency gap: ce75 AC#3 ("epic 5fd2 spec references this rollout/rollback design") would imply a producer→parent edge, but the parent settled the matter independently (SC#4), so no missing live dependency edge. CLEAN.
- Ordering violation: ce75 is closed/superseded and ships nothing into the build order; the risk-ordered MVP spine (012e/ca03/1913/acc1/487d/4bb7/092b) is unaffected. CLEAN.
- Consumer impact: ce75 changes no producer surface beyond the config flag already owned by 092b; no sibling consumer is broken. CLEAN.
- Residual references: ce75 AC#3's "spec references this design" is the only dangling reference; it resolves to the closed-superseded state and is addressed by the suggested superseded_by links above (folded into the finding, not a separate mode). CLEAN otherwise.

G4 NET: one high-confidence consistency finding (conflicting-assumptions vs parent SC#4 + scope-overlap with 092b/db7b, zero net contribution, correctly superseded); the other 5 interaction modes are clean.
